elements.sent-2411-520: We study the systematics related to the assumption of the fiducial cosmology by fitting \desidrone\ Abacus-2 complete mocks with analytic covariance.
elements.sent-2411-521: We first determine whether there is a detection of a systematic effect by computing the maximum shift observed between different cosmologies for all tracers and redshift bins.
elements.sent-2411-522: We express the shifts in terms of the precision of the complete 25 realisations of Abacus.
elements.sent-2411-523: This way we have enough sensitivity (of the volume of 25 realisations) to detect potential systematic shifts that would otherwise be hidden by the statistical precision of just 1 realisation.
elements.sent-2411-524: For shifts that are above the 3$\sigma$ threshold, we report a detection of a systematic.
elements.sent-2411-525: Then, we look at both the relative shifts with respect to the baseline Planck $\Lambda$CDM cosmology and with respect to the truth.
elements.sent-2411-526: We quantify those shifts in terms of DR1 error so we convert the shifts expressed in terms of $\sigma_{\rm V25}$ into shifts expressed in terms of $\sigma_{\rm DR1}$.
elements.sent-2411-527: The analysis and the results are further described in our supporting paper \cite{KP5s8-Gsponer .}
elements.sent-2411-528: For the Full-Modelling approach, we did not detect any systematic effect for any of the tracers if the detection limit is set to 3 $\sigma_{\rm V25}$ (all of them are well below 1 $\sigma_{\rm V25}$).
elements.sent-2411-529: Moreover, the maximal shifts for each tracer is below 0.2 $\sigma_{\rm DR1}$.
elements.sent-2411-530: For ShapeFit parameters, we found that the shape parameter $m$ is the most affected by changes in the fiducial cosmology, with observed shifts that are often above 0.2 $\sigma_{\rm DR1}$.
elements.sent-2411-531: This is consistent with what was reported in \cite{brieden2021 using another set of simulations with a volume that would roughly correspond to the lowest DESI LRG redshift bin.}
elements.sent-2411-532: However, for the parameters of interest ($\alpha_\parallel$,$\alpha_\perp$ and $f\sigma_8$), the observed maximal shifts are below 0.2 $\sigma_{\rm DR1}$.
elements.sent-2411-533: Therefore, we report a common systematic error contribution that is below 0.2 $\sigma_{\rm DR1}$ for both Full-Modelling and ShapeFit in \cref{tab:systematicbudget .}
elements.sent-2411-534: As mentioned in \cref{subsec:methods-2pt , in order to mitigate the impact of fibre incompleteness on the Full-Shape galaxy two-point clustering analysis, we cut angular scales smaller than 0.05 deg.}
elements.sent-2411-535: This method called `$\theta$-cut' was proposed in \cite{KP3s5-Pinon where it was validated against \abacus\ mocks for the ELG sample.}
elements.sent-2411-536: To further validate the $\theta$-cut method for Full-Shape galaxy two-point clustering analysis, we first look at the \desidrone\ Abacus-2 complete mocks that do not have fibre incompleteness and fit the mean of the 25 mocks with and without a $\theta$-cut applied.
elements.sent-2411-537: Then, we look at the relative difference in cosmological parameters inferred from the \desidrone\ \abacus\ `complete' (without fibre incompleteness) and `altmtl' mocks (with fibre incompleteness as implemented into the data).
elements.sent-2411-538: We also fit the mean of the 25 mocks for each tracer and redshift bin.
elements.sent-2411-539: The results of the fits on the mean of 25 mocks for each tracer and redshift bin are shown in \cref{fig:fa-SF for the ShapeFit parameters and in \cref{fig:fa-FM for the Full-Modelling results on $h$, $w_\mathrm{cdm}$, $\sigma_8$ and $\log A_s$.}}
elements.sent-2411-540: In both cases, the figures on the top correspond to the difference between without and with $\theta$-cut on the complete \abacus\ mocks and the bottom figures show the difference between complete and `altmtl' mocks with $\theta$-cut.
elements.sent-2411-541: For the top and bottom figures, we show the MAP results and the bottom panel shows the difference between the two methods divided by the error with the blue shaded region representing $\pm$ 1/5 of DR1 error and the red dashed lines $\pm$ 2/5 of DR1 error.
elements.sent-2411-542: Given that we only have 25 realisations, the intrinsic statistical error is 5 times smaller than the \desidrone\ error, meaning that it is very likely to see a deviation within the shaded region, or in other words we cannot detect a systematic below 0.2$\sigma$ with only 25 mocks.
elements.sent-2411-543: If we focus on the top figures first, the effect of applying (or not) the $\theta$-cut method on complete mocks is negligible, which validates the accuracy of the $\theta$-cut method.
elements.sent-2411-544: Then, if we look at the bottom figures, the comparison between complete and `altmtl' mocks with $\theta$-cut informs us about any potential residual fibre-assignment effect which would not be fully mitigated by the $\theta$-cut method.
elements.sent-2411-545: As expected, we see a larger scatter in that case with some variations within the two red lines but with no systematic trend as a function of a given parameter or tracer.
elements.sent-2411-546: Therefore such deviations do not necessarily correspond to systematic offsets.
elements.sent-2411-547: For example, we see a consistent over-estimation of $\sigma_8$ across all the redshift bins.
elements.sent-2411-548: Moreover, the results from the joint fit, which is the case used for the final cosmological constraints, lie mostly within 1/5 of DR1 error.
elements.sent-2411-549: Therefore, we conclude that the $\theta$-cut method works well enough to mitigate the effect of fibre assignment in DR1 galaxy Full-Shape analysis, and we do not report any residual systematic contribution.
elements.sent-2411-550: For future analysis of the impact of DESI fibre assignment, we will produce mocks with a more realistic fibre-assignment effect that will allow us to test the $\theta$-cut method further and also include the Probability Inverse Pair (PIP) weighting \citep{PIP,Smith19 which should allow us to also fit smaller scales.}
elements.sent-2411-551: Although this was not practical for DR1 samples given the large fraction of the sample with just one-pass regions, it would be possible for DR2, where the one-pass fraction is much smaller.

elements.sent-2411-553: There are three different regression methods that lead to different imaging weights: 1) linear method, as used in the eBOSS LSS catalogues \citep{eBOSSlss , 2) Regressis random forest (RF) \cite{KP3s10-Chaussidon , 3) non-linear SYSNet neural net (NN) \citep{sysnet as used in \cite{2021MNRAS.503.2562Z,2024MNRAS.532.1902R .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-554: Linear weights are sufficient to capture most of the trend in target density with imaging observing conditions for the BGS and LRG samples, which are caused by bright objects.
elements.sent-2411-555: For the ELG and QSO samples, we found that different choices of imaging weights (linear, RF, NN) yield dissimilar clustering measurements, which can impact the cosmological constraints.
elements.sent-2411-556: To handle this we have developed a template correction, with one extra free parameter ($s_p$), allowing us to marginalise over some of the remaining imaging systematic effects in the data.
elements.sent-2411-557: Further, when we ran null tests on mocks uncontaminated by systematics, we found that methods beyond linear regression tend to remove some modes in the signal, which could bias our estimated two-point functions.
elements.sent-2411-558: Thus we also apply a mode removal correction, which is estimated from mocks.
elements.sent-2411-559: For consistency, we decided to test these two corrections on all tracers and study their impact on the clustering and the cosmological results.
elements.sent-2411-560: The effective model used to fit the data is defined by: \begin{eqnarray}\nonumber\mathrm{model}_\mathrm{eff} = \mathrm{theory} + s_p \mathrm{Poly}[(X^{\mathrm{data}}_{\mathrm{weight}} - X^{\mathrm{data}}_{\mathrm{noweight}}) - (X^{\mathrm{mock}}_{\mathrm{weight}} - X^{\mathrm{mock}}_{\mathrm{noweight}})] + + \mathrm{Poly}[X^{\mathrm{mock}}_{\mathrm{weight}} - X^{\mathrm{mock}}_{\mathrm{noweight}}] \label{eq:imaging-corr}\end{eqnarray} where $s_p$ is a free parameter, $\mathrm{Poly}$ is a polynomial function of third order\footnote{We adopted a form of $c_{-5 k^{-5 + c_{-3 k^{-3 + c_{-2 k^{-2}$ for the power spectrum to get a smooth template.}}}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-020: Systematics related to the covariance matrix estimate
elements.sent-2411-580: To estimate the systematics associated with our choice of the covariance matrix, we perform both Full Modelling and ShapeFit fits on \desidrone\, Abacus-2 mocks using either analytic or EZmocks covariance.
elements.sent-2411-581: The results presented in this paper are based on the supporting papers exploring the impact of the RascalC code for generating analytical covariance in configuration space \cite{KP4s7-Rashkovetskyi , TheCov code in Fourier space \cite{KP4s8-Alves , and their application and comparison with mock-based covariance in the context of DESI \cite{KP4s6-Forero-Sanchez .}}}
elements.sent-2411-582: Below we summarise the key findings that set the methodology to account for systematic effects in our baseline EZmocks covariance:.
elements.sent-2411-583: When performing the fits on \abacus\ mocks without fibre-assignment effect, EZmocks and analytic covariances agree very well, both in Fourier and configuration spaces.
elements.sent-2411-584: When performing the fits on \abacus\ mocks with `altmtl' fibre assignment\footnote{This is, using the `altmtl' version as it closely corresponds to the realistic data processing, whereas the fast fibre-assignment procedure is approximate but more efficient, and thus can be applied to hundreds of mocks.}
elements.sent-2411-585: This mismatch is a bit less in configuration space ($\sim5\%$) over the fitting range considered.
elements.sent-2411-586: When we compare the elements of the RascalC analytical covariance matrix (tuned to reproduce the \desidrone\ data) with the EZmocks (with fast-fibreassign) prediction we find that RascalC matrix elements present a higher variance than than EZmocks matrix elements.
elements.sent-2411-587: In other words, we find that the minimum $\chi^2$ in fits to the data is higher when employing the EZmocks covariance than when employing the RascalC one.
elements.sent-2411-588: Thus, we conclude that EZmocks with fast-fibreassign are not able to capture all aspects of the \desidrone, which results in an underestimation of the error bars.
elements.sent-2411-589: This is in part caused by the limited fast-fibre assignment method applied to the mocks, but also in part by the nature of these approximate mocks.
elements.sent-2411-590: For simplicity, we decided to correct them by rescaling all the covariance elements by a tracer-dependent factor previously introduced in \cref{tab:cov-corr .}
elements.sent-2411-591: These are computed by comparing the covariance matrix scaled by the inverse of the reduced chi-squared of RascalC analytical covariance, and the mock-based covariance, both evaluated in configuration space over the range $20 < s < 200 \hinvmpc$, as described in section~10.2 and equation~10.12 of \cite{DESI2024.II.KP3 .}
elements.sent-2411-592: As discussed in \cref{subsec:sys-hod , the baseline Full-Modelling approach for constraining the cosmological parameters makes the contributions from systematic effects at the parameter level more difficult to interpret beyond $\Lambda$CDM models.}
elements.sent-2411-593: In order to avoid repeating the study of systematics for each cosmological model considered, we include the systematic error budget at the data vector level.
elements.sent-2411-594: The final covariance matrix for Full-Shape analysis of the galaxy power spectrum is inferred as follows.
elements.sent-2411-595: The covariance matrix of the galaxy power spectrum monopole and quadrupole elements, ($N_{\rm bins}$), is estimated for each tracer and redshift bin with $N_\mathrm{obs = 1000$ EZmocks.}
elements.sent-2411-596: Then, the corrective factors described in \cref{subsec:sys-cov are applied and are multiplied by the (inverse) Hartlap \cite{Hartlap2007 factor.}}
elements.sent-2411-597: At this point, we could have also followed the Sellentin-Heavens approach \cite{Sellentin-Heavens to obtain an unbiased likelihood, however, for this large number of mock realizations the difference between these two approaches is not significant (see \cite{Gil-Marin2022 for a comparison).}}
elements.sent-2411-598: The Hartlap inverse matrix which gives an unbiased estimate of the inverse covariance matrix is, \begin{equation}C_\mathrm{Hartlap 2007}^{-1} = \frac{N_\mathrm{obs} - 1}{N_\mathrm{obs} - N_\mathrm{bins} - 2} \label{eq:hartlap}\end{equation} and the \cite{Percival2014 correction factor (to propagate uncertainty in the covariance matrix to final cosmological constraints): \begin{equation}C_\mathrm{Percival 2014} = \frac{1 + B (N_\mathrm{bins} - N_\mathrm{params})}{1 + A + B (N_\mathrm{params} - 1)}\end{equation} with \begin{equation}A = \frac{2}{(N_\mathrm{obs} - N_\mathrm{bins} - 1) (N_\mathrm{obs} - N_\mathrm{bins} - 4)}, B = \frac{N_\mathrm{obs} - N_\mathrm{bins} - 2}{(N_\mathrm{obs} - N_\mathrm{bins} - 1) (N_\mathrm{obs} - N_\mathrm{bins} - 4)} \label{eq:percival}\end{equation} assuming $N_\mathrm{params = 7$ parameters (corresponding to bias parameters $(1 + b_1) \sigma_8$, $b_2 \sigma_8^2$, $b_s \sigma_8^2$, counter-terms $\alpha_0$, $\alpha_2$ and stochastic parameters $\mathrm{SN _0$, $\mathrm{SN _2$).\footnote{This results in a conservative estimate: considering 12 parameters instead (e.g. including cosmology parameters) would reduce this factor by $1\%$.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}A = \frac{2}{(N_obs - N_bins - 1) (N_obs - N_bins - 4)}, B = \frac{N_obs - N_bins - 2}{(N_obs - N_bins - 1) (N_obs - N_bins - 4)} \label{eq:percival}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-599: Eventually, to the galaxy power spectrum covariance, we add the systematic covariance matrices, namely:.
elements.sent-2411-600: The total systematic error budget at the parameter level is summarised in \cref{tab:systematicbudget for both Full Modelling and ShapeFit.}
elements.sent-2411-601: Systematic contributions at the level or above 0.2$\sigma$ of DR1 error are added in quadrature to obtain the total systematic error contribution at the parameter level.
elements.sent-2411-602: This includes the effect due to imaging systematics, fibre-assignment, and HOD+PWE, and it results in a total systematic error budget of 0.46$\sigma$ of DR1 error.
elements.sent-2411-603: In \cref{app:invcov , we present details on how the systematic and statistic contributions of the covariance have been added.}

elements.sent-2411-612: From the initial stages of the survey, even before the data was taken or inspected, DESI collaboration decided to imprint a post-processing on the redshifts of the galaxies to distort the true underlying cosmological signal, which we refer as the blinding process, as previously described in \cref{subsec:blinding .}
elements.sent-2411-613: As far as we are aware, this represents the first ever blinded Full-Shape analysis of a galaxy redshift survey.
elements.sent-2411-614: The creation of the large-scale structure catalogues for the six tracer samples, took place over six iterations over 13 months as described in \cite{DESI2024.II.KP3 .}
elements.sent-2411-615: During this evolution, the catalogue remained blinded to avoid confirmation bias, limiting our ability to unconsciously make any changes in the process to reproduce results we feel comfortable with (i.e., results supported by previous observations).
elements.sent-2411-616: The evolution leading to the final six-tracer catalogues of the \desidrone\, included performing a series of tests on our data pipeline, resulting in the inclusion of the latest techniques for correcting for the DESI-related systematics, the imaging and fibre-assignments (see \cref{subsec:sys-imaging and \cref{subsec:sys-fibre ).}}
elements.sent-2411-617: In addition, during this time, the modelling pipeline evolved to include the final prior choice, the range of scales that we fit, and a free parameter which encodes the uncertainty in the imaging weights that is marginalised over in the analysis (see equation~\ref{eq:imaging-corr ).}
elements.sent-2411-618: We instigated a series of tests and conditions that need to be passed before we would consider unblinding the catalogue.
elements.sent-2411-619: These include sanity and consistency checks on the data catalogues, freezing the model pipeline including the perturbation theory model, the priors on the parameters, the fitting ranges, the way that the data covariance is estimated, and the way the systematic error budget is defined.
elements.sent-2411-620: Most of the data tests were performed using ShapeFit instead of the baseline Full-Modelling approach, due to the following: 1) We demonstrated that for a large range of models ShapeFit and Full Modelling can retrieve the same information; 2) ShapeFit does not rely on the assumption of a specific choice of cosmology; 3) ShapeFit is more flexible in identifying potential systematic effects in the shape of the power spectrum, as each compressed variable is associated to a particular range of scales; 4) Since at this point we do not need to make the cosmological interpretation, we can analyse the ShapeFit results in terms of compressed parameters and not suffer from systematic shifts due to the prior volume effects.
elements.sent-2411-621: The full conditions to unblind are listed below and summarised in \cref{tab:Y1checklist .}
elements.sent-2411-622: The official \desione\ galaxy Full-Shape analysis base pipeline should be decided before unblinding.
elements.sent-2411-623: This includes the choice of compressed or full-modelling approaches, the choice of EFT model, the definition of nuisance parameters, their priors and the baseline set-up (fitting range, binning, multipoles considered).
elements.sent-2411-624: The resulting galaxy Full-Shape analysis baseline has been summarised in \cref{subsec:methods-baseline .}
elements.sent-2411-625: The different options for combining the BAO post-reconstruction and the Full-Shape pre-recon measurements should be identified and described before unblinding.
elements.sent-2411-626: Although the final decision on which model to implement can be decided after unblinding, this choice was not based on the unblinded catalogue performance.
elements.sent-2411-627: As previous studies have demonstrated, the different available methodologies perform very consistently.
elements.sent-2411-629: The upper limit of systematic errors has to be determined before unblinding in order to avoid inflating the final error bars such that the final results align better with expectations influenced by confirmation bias.
elements.sent-2411-630: However, the decision and the exact methodology on how to include the systematics at the power spectrum $k$-bins level was developed after unblinding because of its novelty.
elements.sent-2411-631: Each individual source of potential systematic effects is described in detail in \cref{sec:systematics .}
elements.sent-2411-632: Further details on how the power spectrum systematic covariance is defined are in \cite{KP5s7-Findlay .}
elements.sent-2411-633: The minimal set of consistency tests described in the checklist (listed in \cref{tab:Y1checklist ) has to be completed on the blinded data and repeated on unblinded data as a first validation after unblinding.}
elements.sent-2411-634: Only ShapeFit fits are allowed to be performed on blinded data, as performing Full-Modelling fits would inform us about the cosmological interpretation of the results and thus could be broken or be inconsistent with the blinding procedure.
